text:
PAYLENS KNOWLEDGE BASE — Fintech & Payments Fundamentals
Source: RBI reports, NPCI docs, BIS reports, Investopedia (compiled manually)
Last updated: March 2026
=======================================================================

=======================================================================
HOW A PAYMENT ACTUALLY WORKS — END TO END
=======================================================================

When you pay someone online, this is what happens in milliseconds:

STEP 1 — AUTHORIZATION:
- Customer enters card/UPI details
- Payment gateway (Razorpay/Stripe) encrypts and sends to acquiring bank
- Acquiring bank contacts card network (Visa/Mastercard/RuPay)
- Card network contacts issuing bank (your bank)
- Issuing bank checks: sufficient balance? fraud signals? account standing?
- Issuing bank responds: Approved or Declined
- This entire process takes 2-3 seconds

STEP 2 — CAPTURE:
- After authorization, the merchant "captures" the amount
- Money is not yet moved — it is only "held" or "reserved"
- Some gateways auto-capture, others require manual capture

STEP 3 — CLEARING:
- At end of day, all transactions are batched and sent for clearing
- Card networks (Visa, Mastercard) process and reconcile transactions
- This is when the actual transfer instructions are created

STEP 4 — SETTLEMENT (T+2):
- Funds move from issuing bank → card network → acquiring bank → merchant account
- Standard settlement time in India: T+2 (2 business days)
- Razorpay, Stripe, PayPal all follow T+2 by default
- Instant settlement available at extra fee (0.25% to 1.5%)
- RBI is pushing for T+0 (same day) settlement for UPI

UPI (Unified Payments Interface):
- Built by NPCI (National Payments Corporation of India)
- Launched: April 2016
- Works 24x7x365, including holidays
- Transfers money in real time (within seconds)
- Limit: INR 1 lakh per transaction (some banks allow up to 2 lakh)
- No charge for P2P (person to person) transfers
- Merchant payments (P2M): 0% MDR below INR 2,000 (RBI mandate 2020)
- Above INR 2,000: MDR up to 0.3% for RuPay, 0.5-0.9% for Visa/Mastercard via UPI
- Daily transaction limit: INR 1 lakh (varies by bank)
- Monthly UPI transactions in India: 10+ billion (as of 2024)

IMPS (Immediate Payment Service):
- Real-time 24x7 transfers
- Limit: INR 5 lakh per transaction
- Charges: INR 5 to INR 25 per transaction (bank dependent)
- Used for: instant bank-to-bank transfers
- Identification: by mobile number + MMID, or account number + IFSC

NEFT (National Electronic Funds Transfer):
- Batch-based settlement (every 30 minutes, 24x7)
- No upper limit (as of 2020 RBI removed the limit)
- Charges: INR 0 to INR 25 depending on amount
- Below INR 10,000: INR 2.50
- INR 10,000 to 1 lakh: INR 5
- INR 1 lakh to 2 lakh: INR 15
- Above INR 2 lakh: INR 25
- Free for online banking (banks cannot charge for NEFT via internet/mobile)

RTGS (Real Time Gross Settlement):
- Minimum transfer: INR 2 lakh
- Settles transaction by transaction in real time (gross settlement)
- Used for large value transfers
- Available: 7 AM to 6 PM on working days
- Charges: INR 25 to INR 50 per transaction

SWIFT (Society for Worldwide Interbank Financial Telecommunication):
- Used for international wire transfers
- NOT a payment network — it's a messaging system between banks
- Banks send SWIFT messages instructing fund transfers
- Takes 2-5 business days for international transfers
- Fees: INR 500 to INR 2,500 per transfer + correspondent bank charges
- Used by: PayPal withdrawals, wire transfers from Outlier/foreign clients

VISA:
- Global card network
- Processes transactions between issuing and acquiring banks
- Takes a fee from each transaction (interchange fee)
- India: ~55% of credit card market
- Supports: credit, debit, prepaid cards

MASTERCARD:
- Second largest card network globally
- Similar to Visa in function
- India: ~35% of credit card market

MASTERCARD:
- Second largest card network globally
- Similar to Visa in function
- India: ~35% of credit card market

RUPAY:
- India's domestic card network, built by NPCI
- Cheaper for merchants (lower MDR)
- Accepted mainly in India
- Cannot be used internationally (mostly)
- Promoted heavily by government for financial inclusion
- Zero MDR for transactions at government merchants

AMERICAN EXPRESS:
- Premium card network
- Higher fees than Visa/Mastercard
- Smaller acceptance network in India
- Higher rewards for cardholders offset by higher merchant fees

MDR (Merchant Discount Rate) — How it splits:
Total MDR = Issuing bank share + Card network share + Acquiring bank share + Payment gateway share
Example for a 2% MDR transaction of INR 1000:
- Issuing bank gets: ~1.1% (INR 11)
- Card network gets: ~0.1% (INR 1)
- Acquiring bank + gateway gets: ~0.8% (INR 8)
- Total: INR 20 deducted from merchant

PAYMENT GATEWAY (e.g., Razorpay, Stripe, PayPal):
- The technology layer that handles the transaction
- Encrypts card data
- Communicates with card networks
- Provides the checkout experience
- Charges MDR/transaction fee for this service

PAYMENT PROCESSOR:
- Executes the actual transfer of funds
- Often the same entity as the gateway in modern setups
- Handles clearing and settlement

ACQUIRING BANK:
- The bank of the merchant
- Holds the merchant account
- Receives funds on behalf of merchant
- Often works behind the payment gateway

ISSUING BANK:
- The bank that issued the customer's card/UPI account
- Approves or declines transactions
- Bears fraud risk (to some extent)

PCI-DSS (Payment Card Industry Data Security Standard):
- Security standard for any entity that stores/processes/transmits card data
- Set by major card networks (Visa, Mastercard, Amex, Discover)
- 12 requirements covering network security, encryption, access control, monitoring
- Compliance required for ALL merchants and payment service providers
- Non-compliance can result in fines of $5,000-$100,000/month

Why it matters for ChargeClarity / Pine Labs:
- Pine Labs handles card transactions for thousands of merchants
- All data must be encrypted and stored securely
- This is why payment gateway APIs don't return full card numbers
- Tokenization: card numbers replaced with tokens to avoid storing sensitive data

What is a chargeback?
- When a customer disputes a charge directly with their bank (not the merchant)
- The bank reverses the transaction and takes money back from the merchant
- Merchant also pays a chargeback fee (USD 15-25 depending on gateway)

Chargeback process:
1. Customer contacts bank, claims unauthorized/wrong charge
2. Bank reverses transaction immediately (provisional credit to customer)
3. Bank notifies payment gateway with a dispute
4. Merchant has 7-14 days to provide evidence (receipt, delivery proof, etc.)
5. If merchant wins: money returned, chargeback fee sometimes refunded
6. If merchant loses: transaction amount + fee permanently gone

Chargeback fees by platform:
- PayPal: USD 20 per dispute (non-refundable even if you win)
- Stripe: USD 15 per dispute (refunded if you win)
- Razorpay: INR 1,250 approximately

High chargeback rate consequences:
- If merchant chargeback rate exceeds 1% of transactions: warning
- If exceeds 2%: account termination risk
- Added to MATCH list (black list) — very hard to get another gateway

How payment gateways detect fraud:
- Velocity checks: same card used multiple times in short window
- Geolocation: IP address vs billing address mismatch
- Device fingerprinting: suspicious device signals
- Machine learning models: pattern matching against known fraud patterns
- CVV/OTP verification: extra authentication layer
- 3D Secure (3DS): OTP sent to customer's registered mobile

India specific: RBI mandates 2FA (Two Factor Authentication) for all online transactions
- Card number + CVV is not enough
- OTP to registered mobile is mandatory for most transactions
- This is why Indian users get OTP prompts for every online payment

Stripe Radar (free with all Stripe accounts):
- ML-based fraud detection
- Blocks suspicious transactions automatically
- Generates a risk score for each transaction
- Advanced Radar: USD 0.02/transaction additional for more controls

Paytm Wallet / MobiKwik / PhonePe Wallet:
- Prepaid Payment Instruments (PPI) regulated by RBI
- Load money from bank, spend from wallet
- P2P transfers: free up to INR 10,000/month, 1% above that
- Merchant payments: similar to UPI
- Full KYC wallet: up to INR 1 lakh balance
- Minimum KYC wallet: up to INR 10,000 balance

Loading wallet fees:
- From bank account/UPI: free
- From credit card: 1.5% to 2% fee typically

Major players: ZestMoney, LazyPay, Simpl, Amazon Pay Later, Flipkart Pay Later
How it works:
- Customer pays nothing at checkout
- BNPL provider pays merchant immediately (minus 2-3% fee)
- Customer repays BNPL provider in installments
- If customer defaults: BNPL provider bears the risk (not the merchant)
Merchant cost: 2-3% of transaction (similar to credit card)
Growing rapidly in India: USD 3.7 billion market as of 2024

WHAT IS AN EXCHANGE RATE?
The price of one currency expressed in terms of another.
Example: 1 USD = 83.50 INR means you need 83.50 Indian Rupees to buy 1 US Dollar.

THE MID-MARKET RATE (also called interbank rate):
- The true "fair" exchange rate between two currencies
- The midpoint between the buy price and sell price
- This is the rate you see on Google or xe.com
- Banks and payment companies NEVER give you this rate
- They add a spread (their profit) on top

BID / ASK SPREAD — How Banks and Gateways Make Money on Forex:
- BID price: the rate at which the bank/gateway BUYS foreign currency from you
- ASK price: the rate at which the bank/gateway SELLS foreign currency to you
- The difference (spread) is the bank's profit
- Example:
  Mid-market rate: 1 USD = 83.50 INR
  PayPal's bid (they buy USD from you): 1 USD = 80.90 INR (3% below mid-market)
  PayPal keeps: 83.50 - 80.90 = INR 2.60 per dollar as profit
  On USD 100 received: PayPal keeps INR 260 just from currency conversion

WHY EXCHANGE RATES FLUCTUATE:
- Interest rates: Higher interest rates → stronger currency (attracts investment)
- Inflation: Higher inflation → weaker currency (reduces purchasing power)
- Economic growth: Strong GDP growth → stronger currency
- Trade balance: More exports than imports → stronger currency
- Political stability: Instability → weaker currency (investors flee)
- Foreign investment: FDI/FII flows affect demand for INR
- RBI intervention: RBI buys/sells USD to stabilize INR

THE INR/USD RATE:
- India runs a current account deficit (imports more than exports)
- This creates natural downward pressure on INR
- RBI intervenes regularly to prevent excessive depreciation
- INR has weakened from ~45/USD (2010) to ~84/USD (2024)
- This means Indians receiving USD payments get MORE INR for the same USD amount

HOW PAYPAL CONVERTS CURRENCY:
1. Transaction arrives in USD (from Outlier, US client, etc.)
2. PayPal checks its internal exchange rate (typically 3% worse than mid-market)
3. PayPal converts and credits INR to your PayPal wallet
4. You withdraw INR to bank account (free if above minimum)

Total PayPal cost on receiving USD 100 from Outlier:
- Transaction fee: 4.4% = USD 4.40 deducted first
- Remaining: USD 95.60
- Currency conversion: PayPal rate ~3% below mid-market
  If mid-market = 83.50 INR/USD, PayPal gives ~81.00 INR/USD
  USD 95.60 × 81.00 = INR 7,743.60
  If you had received at mid-market: USD 95.60 × 83.50 = INR 7,982.60
  Lost to conversion: INR 239
- Total cost on USD 100: INR 367 (transaction fee) + INR 239 (conversion) = INR 606
- Effective rate: you received INR 7,744 instead of INR 8,350 = 7.3% total loss

HOW BANKS CONVERT CURRENCY (for wire transfers):
- Banks have their own treasury rates
- Typically 1-2% spread above mid-market (better than PayPal's 3%)
- Plus SWIFT fees of INR 500-2,500 per transfer
- For large amounts, wire transfer may be cheaper overall than PayPal

STRIPE CURRENCY CONVERSION:
- Stripe charges 2% additional for currency conversion (on top of transaction fee)
- This is more transparent than PayPal's bundled spread
- Example: 3% (international card) + 2% (conversion) = 5% total
- Stripe uses a fairer exchange rate (close to mid-market)

FEMA (Foreign Exchange Management Act, 1999):
- Governs all foreign exchange transactions in India
- Administered by RBI
- Violations can result in fines up to 3x the amount involved

KEY RULES FOR FREELANCERS / OUTLIER CONTRIBUTORS:
- You CAN legally receive foreign payments for services rendered
- Must be received within 9 months of export of service
- Must be repatriated (brought into India) — cannot keep abroad indefinitely
- Foreign currency in your account must be converted to INR eventually

HOW TO RECEIVE FOREIGN PAYMENTS LEGALLY:
Option 1 — PayPal:
  - Register as business/freelancer
  - Link Indian bank account
  - Receive payment → withdraw to bank
  - PayPal handles compliance automatically

Option 2 — Wire Transfer (SWIFT):
  - Give client your bank account + IFSC + SWIFT code
  - Direct bank-to-bank transfer
  - Lower fees for large amounts
  - Bank handles FEMA compliance

Option 3 — Wise (formerly TransferWise):
  - Mid-market exchange rate
  - Flat fee (much cheaper than PayPal/bank)
  - Fully compliant with FEMA
  - Recommended for regular freelancers receiving foreign income

Option 4 — Payment platforms like Payoneer:
  - Gives you a virtual US bank account
  - Clients pay in USD to your US account
  - You withdraw to Indian bank in INR
  - 2% withdrawal fee, good exchange rate

LRS (Liberalised Remittance Scheme):
- Allows Indians to SEND up to USD 250,000 per financial year abroad
- For education, travel, gifts, investment, etc.
- RECEIVING money from abroad is not subject to LRS limits
- LRS is about outward remittances, not inward

TCS (Tax Collected at Source) on Foreign Remittances:
- TCS is applicable when you SEND money abroad under LRS
- Rate: 5% TCS on amounts above INR 7 lakh per year (as of 2023 RBI notification)
- For foreign education loan: 0.5% TCS
- This TCS is a pre-payment of tax, credited against your tax liability
- If you're RECEIVING money: TCS does NOT apply to inward remittances

PURPOSE CODE FOR RECEIVING PAYMENTS:
- When receiving foreign payments, Indian banks ask for "Purpose Code"
- For freelance services / exports: Purpose Code P0802 (Software services)
- For other professional services: Purpose Code P0699
- Bank processes FIRC (Foreign Inward Remittance Certificate) automatically
- FIRC is proof of foreign payment received — keep it safe for taxes

TAXES ON FOREIGN INCOME IN INDIA:
- Foreign freelance income is taxable in India
- Add to your total income under "Profits and Gains from Business/Profession"
- Eligible deductions: internet, equipment, software, home office
- File ITR-3 or ITR-4 (presumptive taxation)
- GST registration required if annual income > INR 20 lakh
  (For export of services: GST = 0% but registration still needed above threshold)

WHAT IS PPP?
A theory that exchange rates should adjust so that identical goods cost the same in different countries.

EXAMPLE:
If a coffee costs USD 5 in the US and INR 100 in India:
PPP exchange rate = 100/5 = 20 INR/USD
But actual market rate = 83.50 INR/USD
This means India's price level is much lower than the US

WHY PPP MATTERS FOR INDIAN FREELANCERS:
- USD 100/hour in the US = INR 8,350 in India at market rates
- But INR 8,350 buys MORE in India than USD 100 buys in the US
- This is why Indian freelancers can charge less than US counterparts
- But also why platforms like Outlier may pay Indian contributors less
- "Purchasing power" of INR 8,350 ≈ USD 200+ in US purchasing power terms

BIG MAC INDEX:
- The Economist's informal PPP measure
- A Big Mac costs ~USD 5.58 in USA, ~INR 230 (USD 2.76) in India
- Implies INR is undervalued by ~50% vs USD by this measure

INFLATION IN INDIA:
- Target: RBI maintains CPI inflation target of 4% (+/- 2%)
- Actual: India's inflation has ranged 4-8% in recent years
- Effect: As inflation rises, INR buys less → exchange rate weakens

REPO RATE (RBI's main policy tool):
- The rate at which RBI lends to commercial banks
- Higher repo rate → borrowing costlier → less money in system → lower inflation
- Lower repo rate → cheaper borrowing → more money → higher growth
- Current repo rate: check RBI website (changes periodically)

EFFECT ON PAYMENT FEES:
- Inflation increases operating costs for payment companies
- Fee increases in payment processing often lag inflation
- Currency depreciation means foreign USD payments are worth more INR over time
  (USD 100 in 2010 = INR 4,500; same USD 100 in 2024 = INR 8,350)

KEY NUMBERS (2024):
- UPI transactions: 13+ billion per month
- Digital payment value: USD 3+ trillion annually
- India accounts for 46% of global real-time payment transactions
- Smartphone penetration: 700+ million users
- Internet users: 900+ million

REGULATORY BODIES:
- RBI (Reserve Bank of India): Regulates all payment systems, banks, NBFCs
- NPCI: Operates UPI, RuPay, IMPS, NACH, AePS
- SEBI: Regulates investment platforms, mutual funds
- IRDAI: Regulates insurance payments
- Ministry of Finance: Policy oversight

KEY REGULATIONS AFFECTING PAYMENT FEES:
- Zero MDR on UPI (RBI mandate, 2020): Merchants cannot be charged for UPI transactions
- Mandatory 2FA for all online card transactions
- PPI interoperability: Wallets must support UPI payments
- Data localisation: All payment data must be stored in India
- KYC requirements: Full KYC for accounts above certain thresholds

FINTECH INNOVATION IN INDIA:
- Account Aggregator framework: Consented financial data sharing
- ONDC (Open Network for Digital Commerce): Open e-commerce protocol
- UPI 123Pay: UPI for feature phones (no internet required)
- UPI Lite: Offline UPI transactions up to INR 200
- Central Bank Digital Currency (CBDC / Digital Rupee): RBI piloting since 2022

PAYLENS KNOWLEDGE BASE — India Tax & Compliance for Freelancers
Source: Income Tax India, GST Council, CA community resources (all public)
Last updated: March 2026
=======================================================================

=======================================================================
GST ON PAYMENT PROCESSING FEES
=======================================================================

GST RATE ON FINANCIAL SERVICES: 18%
This applies to ALL payment processing fees in India.

HOW IT AFFECTS YOU:
- Razorpay charges 2% base fee → 18% GST on that fee → Total = 2.36%
- Stripe charges 2% base fee → 18% GST on that fee → Total = 2.36%
- Example on INR 10,000 transaction:
  Base fee = INR 200
  GST on fee = INR 36
  Total deducted = INR 236 (not INR 200)

GST ON PAYPAL:
- PayPal is an international service
- PayPal does NOT charge GST separately — it's embedded in the conversion spread
- If you're a GST-registered business, you cannot claim input credit on PayPal fees
- Stripe and Razorpay DO provide GST invoices → you CAN claim input credit

IS FREELANCE INCOME TAXABLE?
Yes. 100%. Under "Profits and Gains from Business or Profession" (PGBP).

TAX SLABS (New Regime FY 2024-25):
- Up to INR 3 lakh: 0%
- INR 3-7 lakh: 5%
- INR 7-10 lakh: 10%
- INR 10-12 lakh: 15%
- INR 12-15 lakh: 20%
- Above INR 15 lakh: 30%
- Rebate: No tax if total income below INR 7 lakh (Section 87A)

DEDUCTIONS AVAILABLE FOR FREELANCERS:
- Internet connection charges: 100% deductible
- Computer/laptop/equipment: Depreciation deductible
- Software subscriptions: 100% deductible
- Home office (proportion of rent/electricity): Partially deductible
- Professional development (courses, books): Deductible
- Payment processing fees (Stripe/Razorpay fees): Deductible as business expense

PRESUMPTIVE TAXATION (Section 44ADA) — GREAT FOR FREELANCERS:
- If professional income < INR 75 lakh per year
- You declare 50% of gross receipts as profit automatically
- No need to maintain detailed books of accounts
- Pay tax on 50% of income, rest assumed to be expenses
- Example: Earned INR 10 lakh → Taxable income = INR 5 lakh
- File ITR-4 for this scheme

TDS FROM INDIAN PLATFORMS:
- If a company (e.g., Indian client) pays you > INR 30,000 in a year for professional services
- They must deduct TDS @ 10% (Section 194J)
- They give you Form 16A
- You claim this TDS credit while filing ITR

TDS FROM FOREIGN CLIENTS (Outlier, US companies):
- Foreign companies are NOT required to deduct Indian TDS
- You receive full payment, NO TDS deducted
- You must calculate and pay advance tax yourself quarterly
- Advance tax due dates: 15 Jun (15%), 15 Sep (45%), 15 Dec (75%), 15 Mar (100%)
- If you miss advance tax and pay later: Interest @ 1% per month (Section 234B/C)

INWARD REMITTANCE TAX TREATMENT:
- Money received from abroad for services = Export of Services
- If GST registered: 0% GST on export of services (zero-rated)
- Income tax: Still applicable (income is taxable regardless of source)
- FIRC (Foreign Inward Remittance Certificate): Your proof of foreign income
  → Keep all FIRCs — needed for tax assessment

WHEN IS GST REGISTRATION MANDATORY?
- If total annual income exceeds INR 20 lakh (services)
- If providing services to overseas clients: Registration required if income > INR 20 lakh
- Special category states (NE states, Uttarakhand, etc.): INR 10 lakh threshold

EXPORT OF SERVICES — GST TREATMENT:
- Services provided to overseas clients = Export of Services
- Export of Services = Zero-Rated Supply under GST
- Zero-rated means GST rate = 0%, but you can claim refund of GST paid on inputs
- Example: You pay 18% GST on software subscriptions → you can claim refund

GSTIN BENEFITS FOR FREELANCERS:
- Can claim input tax credit on business expenses (Razorpay fees, software, etc.)
- Required for invoicing to Indian companies above threshold
- Appears professional to clients
- Easier compliance vs informal cash dealings

HOW TO FILE:
- GSTR-1: Monthly/quarterly sales return (export invoices)
- GSTR-3B: Monthly summary return (even if zero sales)
- LUT (Letter of Undertaking): File annually to export services without paying GST
  (Without LUT, you pay 18% GST and later claim refund — more hassle)

1. OPEN A SEPARATE BANK ACCOUNT for all freelance income
   - Makes tracking easy for tax purposes
   - Separates personal and business money

2. TRACK ALL PAYMENT FEES as deductible expenses
   - PayPal: Take screenshots of fee deductions monthly
   - Stripe/Razorpay: Download monthly statements
   - These are legitimate business expenses

3. DOCUMENT YOUR FIRC/PAYMENT PROOF
   - Every international payment should generate an FIRC from your bank
   - Ask your bank for FIRC or download from net banking
   - Required for LRS compliance and potential tax scrutiny

4. QUARTERLY ADVANCE TAX (if income > INR 1 lakh/year from freelancing)
   - Estimate annual income
   - Pay 15%/45%/75%/100% by the due dates
   - Avoids interest penalties

5. MAINTAIN SIMPLE BOOKS:
   - Date of payment
   - Amount in foreign currency
   - Amount converted to INR (exchange rate)
   - Platform fees deducted
   - Net amount received
   - Excel sheet is sufficient for presumptive taxation scheme

6. CONSIDER HIRING A CA ONCE YOU CROSS INR 5 LAKH ANNUAL INCOME
   - Cost: INR 3,000 to 10,000 per year for basic filing
   - Worth it for compliance peace of mind
   - Can find one at ClearTax, IndiaFilings, or local CA

MISTAKE 1: Not reporting foreign income
- All foreign income is taxable in India
- Income Tax dept can track inward remittances through banking data
- Penalty for under-reporting: 50% to 200% of tax due

MISTAKE 2: Confusing TCS and TDS
- TCS (Tax Collected at Source): On outward remittances (sending money abroad)
- TDS (Tax Deducted at Source): Indian clients deduct from payments to you
- Foreign clients do NOT deduct Indian TDS

MISTAKE 3: Not saving FIRCs
- FIRC is your proof of export of services
- Required if GST department asks for proof
- Keep digital copies of all FIRCs for 7 years

MISTAKE 4: Thinking PayPal fees are not taxable expenses
- PayPal fees are legitimate business expenses
- Document: Amount received before fees, fees deducted, net received
- Deductible under PGBP (Profits and Gains from Business/Profession)

MISTAKE 5: Not filing advance tax on time
- Even if you're in 0% slab, file advance tax return as nil if applicable
- Non-filing can attract notices even for zero tax

CHARGECLARITY KNOWLEDGE BASE — Payment Platform Fees
Source: Official fee pages (PayPal, Stripe, Razorpay) — compiled manually
Last updated: March 2026
=======================================================================

=======================================================================
PAYPAL INDIA — FEE STRUCTURE
Source: https://www.paypal.com/in/webapps/mpp/paypal-fees
=======================================================================

DOMESTIC TRANSACTIONS (sender and receiver both in India):
- Sending money to friends/family (personal): No fee if funded by PayPal balance or bank account
- Receiving money for goods/services (commercial): 2.5% of transaction amount

INTERNATIONAL TRANSACTIONS (sender and receiver in different countries):
- Definition: A transaction where sender and receiver are registered in different markets/countries
- Base transaction fee: 4.4% of the amount received
- Fixed fee: Varies by currency (e.g., 0.30 USD for USD transactions)
- Additional currency conversion fee: 3% to 4% above the base exchange rate

CURRENCY CONVERSION:
- PayPal adds a fee ABOVE the base exchange rate (mid-market rate)
- For most currencies: 3% above base exchange rate
- For some currencies: 4% above base exchange rate
- Example: If base rate is 1 USD = 83 INR, PayPal rate may be 1 USD = 80.5 INR (approx 3% spread)
- This conversion fee is how PayPal makes money on currency exchange

RECEIVING PAYMENTS FROM ABROAD (e.g., from Outlier AI, US clients):
- You pay: 4.4% transaction fee + fixed fee + currency conversion spread
- Example: Receive USD 100 → PayPal deducts ~4.4% = USD 4.40 → then converts remaining at 3% below market rate
- Total effective cost: approximately 7-8% of the received amount

PAYPAL WITHDRAWAL TO INDIAN BANK:
- Withdrawing INR to Indian bank: Free (if above minimum threshold)
- Minimum withdrawal: INR 100
- Processing time: 3-5 business days

PAYPAL DISPUTE/CHARGEBACK FEE:
- If a buyer files a claim and wins: Seller pays USD 20 (or local equivalent) chargeback fee
- This is in addition to refunding the transaction amount

STANDARD PRICING (Pay-as-you-go):
- Cards issued in India: 2% per transaction
- Cards issued outside India (international cards): 3% per transaction
- No setup fees, no monthly fees, no hidden fees
- No closure fees

CURRENCY CONVERSION:
- If currency conversion is required: Additional 2% fee on top of transaction fee
- Example: Indian customer paying with USD card → 3% (international) + 2% (conversion) = 5% total

SPECIFIC PAYMENT METHODS:
- UPI payments in India: 2% per transaction (same as domestic cards)
- Net Banking: 2% per transaction
- Cards (Visa/Mastercard/RuPay issued in India): 2% per transaction
- American Express: Higher rates apply (contact Stripe)

FAILED PAYMENTS:
- Stripe does NOT charge for failed payment attempts
- You only pay fees on successful transactions

DISPUTES AND CHARGEBACKS:
- Dispute fee: USD 15 per dispute (approximately INR 1,250)
- Fee is refunded if you win the dispute
- Dispute fee applies regardless of transaction currency

PAYOUTS (receiving your Stripe balance):
- Standard payout to Indian bank: Free
- Payout schedule: T+2 (2 business days after transaction)
- Instant payouts: Available for eligible accounts, small fee applies

STRIPE RADAR (Fraud detection):
- Included free with standard pricing
- Advanced Radar: USD 0.02 per transaction additional fee

STANDARD PRICING:
- All domestic transactions: 2% per transaction
- International cards: 3% per transaction
- No setup fee for standard account

PAYMENT METHOD BREAKDOWN:
- UPI (BHIM, Google Pay, PhonePe via Razorpay): 0% fee (as per NPCI guidelines, UPI is free for peer-to-peer; merchant rates apply for business)
- Net Banking: 2% per transaction
- Credit/Debit Cards (Domestic): 2% per transaction
- Credit/Debit Cards (International): 3% per transaction
- Wallets (Paytm, MobiKwik, etc.): 2% per transaction
- EMI transactions: 2% + EMI processing fee

GST ON FEES:
- All Razorpay fees are subject to 18% GST
- Example: 2% fee on INR 1000 = INR 20 → GST on INR 20 = INR 3.60 → Total deducted = INR 23.60

INSTANT SETTLEMENTS:
- Standard settlement: T+2 (2 business days)
- Instant settlement available: 0.25% to 1.5% additional fee depending on plan
- Razorpay X (current account): Free instant settlement

REFUNDS:
- Full refunds: No fee charged (Razorpay refunds the transaction fee too)
- Partial refunds: Transaction fee not refunded

INTERNATIONAL PAYMENTS ACCEPTANCE:
- Accept international cards: 3% fee
- Requires additional KYC and activation
- Currency conversion handled by bank at prevailing rates

PEER-TO-PEER (P2P) UPI TRANSFERS:
- Sending money to a person via UPI (Google Pay, PhonePe, BHIM): FREE
- No transaction fee for amounts up to INR 1 lakh per transaction
- No daily limit fee

MERCHANT PAYMENTS (P2M) via UPI:
- Transactions below INR 2,000: FREE (0% MDR as per RBI mandate)
- Transactions above INR 2,000: Up to 0.3% MDR (Merchant Discount Rate) may apply
- Prepaid Payment Instruments (PPI wallets) via UPI: 1.1% MDR above INR 2,000

FAILED TRANSACTION REVERSAL:
- Failed UPI transactions: Amount reversed within 2-3 business days
- No charge for failed transactions
- Bank may charge for excess failed transaction attempts in some cases

MDR (Merchant Discount Rate):
- The fee a merchant pays to accept digital payments
- Charged as a percentage of each transaction
- Split between payment gateway, acquiring bank, and card network

Transaction Exchange Rate:
- The rate at which PayPal/Stripe converts your money
- Always slightly worse than the mid-market (interbank) rate
- The difference between mid-market rate and the offered rate IS the currency conversion fee

Chargeback:
- When a customer disputes a transaction with their bank
- Bank reverses the charge and the merchant is debited
- Merchant also pays a chargeback fee to the payment gateway

Settlement:
- When the payment gateway transfers collected money to your bank account
- Standard settlement: T+2 (2 business days after transaction date)
- Instant settlement: Same day or next day, usually for an extra fee

PPI (Prepaid Payment Instrument):
- Prepaid wallets like Paytm, MobiKwik
- Loaded in advance and used for payments
- Subject to different fee structures than bank-linked UPI

GST on Payment Fees:
- All payment processing fees in India attract 18% GST
- This is charged ON TOP of the base fee
- Example: 2% base fee on INR 10,000 = INR 200 → GST = INR 36 → Total fee = INR 236

PAYPAL INDIA — FEE STRUCTURE
Source: PayPal India official fee page

========================================
PAYPAL FEES FOR INDIA (RECEIVING PAYMENTS)
========================================
Receiving from within India:
  Standard: 2.5% + ₹3 per transaction

Receiving international payments (export/freelance):
  Standard commercial rate: 4.4% + fixed fee
  Fixed fee by currency:
    USD: $0.30
    GBP: £0.20
    EUR: €0.35
    CAD: $0.30

TOTAL EFFECTIVE DEDUCTION on international payment:
  Currency conversion: ~3-4% spread over mid-market rate
  Transaction fee: 4.4% + fixed fee
  Total loss: ~7-8% on international payments is common

PayPal Currency Conversion:
  PayPal applies a currency conversion spread (markup) over the base exchange rate.
  This is typically 3% to 4% above the mid-market rate.
  This is separate from the transaction fee.

WhY PayPal Feels Expensive:
  1. Transaction fee (4.4%)
  2. Currency conversion markup (3-4%)
  3. Fixed per-transaction fee ($0.30 for USD)
  Combined, receiving USD 100 → you may get ₹7,800-8,100 instead of ~₹8,350

PayPal Withdrawal to Indian Bank Account:
  Withdrawal fee: FREE (no fee to withdraw to Indian bank)
  Time: 3-5 business days

PayPal GST in India:
  18% GST applies on PayPal's service fees (not on the transaction amount)

PayPal GST in India:
  18% GST applies on PayPal's service fees (not on the transaction amount)

Alternatives for freelancers receiving international payments:
  Wise (TransferWise): ~0.5-1% fee, mid-market rate
  Payoneer: Free for same-currency transfers, 2% for USD withdrawal
  Razorpay: For domestic business payments

PCI-DSS — PAYMENT CARD INDUSTRY DATA SECURITY STANDARD
Source: PCI Security Standards Council (PCI SSC)
Current Version: PCI DSS v4.0 (released March 2022, mandatory since March 2024)

========================================
WHAT IS PCI-DSS?
========================================
PCI-DSS (Payment Card Industry Data Security Standard) is a set of security
standards designed to ensure that ALL companies that accept, process, store,
or transmit credit card information maintain a secure environment.

Created by: Visa, Mastercard, American Express, Discover, JCB (the major card brands)
Governed by: PCI Security Standards Council (PCI SSC)
Applies to: Any entity handling cardholder data — merchants, processors, gateways

========================================
PCI-DSS COMPLIANCE LEVELS
========================================
Level 1: Over 6 million card transactions per year
  - Annual on-site audit by Qualified Security Assessor (QSA)
  - Quarterly network scan by Approved Scanning Vendor (ASV)

Level 2: 1 to 6 million transactions per year
  - Annual Self-Assessment Questionnaire (SAQ)
  - Quarterly network scan

Level 3: 20,000 to 1 million e-commerce transactions per year
  - Annual SAQ
  - Quarterly network scan

Level 4: Fewer than 20,000 e-commerce OR up to 1 million other transactions
  - Annual SAQ
  - Quarterly network scan (recommended)

========================================
12 PCI-DSS REQUIREMENTS
========================================
1. Install and maintain a firewall to protect cardholder data
2. Do not use vendor-supplied defaults for passwords and security parameters
3. Protect stored cardholder data (encryption, masking)
4. Encrypt transmission of cardholder data across open, public networks
5. Use and regularly update anti-virus software
6. Develop and maintain secure systems and applications
7. Restrict access to cardholder data on a need-to-know basis
8. Assign a unique ID to each person with computer access
9. Restrict physical access to cardholder data
10. Track and monitor all access to network resources and cardholder data
11. Regularly test security systems and processes
12. Maintain an information security policy

========================================
PCI-DSS IN INDIA — CONTEXT
========================================
RBI mandates: All payment aggregators and gateways in India must be
PCI-DSS certified as per RBI Payment Aggregator guidelines (2020).

Razorpay: PCI-DSS Level 1 certified
PayU: PCI-DSS Level 1 certified
Paytm Payment Gateway: PCI-DSS certified
Stripe India: PCI-DSS Level 1 certified
PayPal India: PCI-DSS Level 1 certified

COST OF NON-COMPLIANCE:
  Card brand fines: $5,000 to $100,000 per month
  Increased transaction fees by acquiring banks
  Risk of losing ability to process card payments
  Data breach liability — average breach cost $4.45 million globally

========================================
TOKENIZATION AND PCI-DSS
========================================
Tokenization: Replacing card number with a non-sensitive equivalent (token)
Reduces PCI-DSS scope significantly — tokenized data is not cardholder data
All major Indian payment gateways use tokenization as per RBI mandate.
RBI Card-on-File tokenization mandate: Effective October 1, 2022.

RAZORPAY — FEE STRUCTURE (INDIA)
Source: Razorpay official pricing page
Category: Payment Gateway — India's leading payment gateway

========================================
RAZORPAY STANDARD FEES
========================================
Domestic Cards (Debit/Credit):
  Standard: 2% per transaction
  International cards: 3% per transaction

UPI Transactions:
  UPI (P2M): 0% — FREE as per RBI mandate
  UPI Autopay: 0.10% for recurring mandates

Net Banking:
  Most banks: ₹15 flat per transaction (not percentage-based)

Wallets:
  Paytm, PhonePe, Amazon Pay: 2% per transaction

EMI Transactions:
  Bank EMI: 2% per transaction (EMI plans by bank)
  Cardless EMI: 3% per transaction

International Payments (via Razorpay):
  3% + currency conversion fee

Settlement Timeline:
  Standard: T+2 (2 business days after transaction)
  Early settlement: Available for fee (Razorpay RazorpayX)

GST: 18% applied on all Razorpay fees
Example: ₹10,000 transaction at 2% = ₹200 fee + ₹36 GST = ₹236 total deduction
You receive: ₹9,764

Razorpay minimum fee: No minimum
Razorpay monthly fee: ₹0 (no monthly subscription for standard plan)

========================================
RAZORPAY PAYMENT LINKS / PAGES
========================================
Standard transaction fee applies
No additional fee for payment links
No setup cost

========================================
RAZORPAY INTERNATIONAL ACCEPTANCE
========================================
Supports 100+ currencies
International cards: 3% + payment gateway fee
Cross-currency settlements: Available in USD, EUR, GBP, SGD, AED

RBI PAYMENT SYSTEMS — FEE STRUCTURE FOR INDIA
Source: Reserve Bank of India (RBI) Official Circulars and NPCI Guidelines
Last updated: 2024

========================================
NEFT — NATIONAL ELECTRONIC FUNDS TRANSFER
========================================
Full form: National Electronic Funds Transfer
Operated by: Reserve Bank of India (RBI)
Settlement: Deferred Net Settlement — processes in half-hourly batches
Availability: 24x7x365 (since December 16, 2019)
Channels: Internet banking, mobile banking, bank branches

NEFT CHARGES — OUTWARD TRANSACTIONS (customer sending money):
  Transaction up to ₹10,000       : ₹2.50 + applicable GST
  Transaction ₹10,001 to ₹1,00,000 : ₹5.00 + applicable GST
  Transaction ₹1,00,001 to ₹2,00,000: ₹15.00 + applicable GST
  Transaction above ₹2,00,000     : ₹25.00 + applicable GST

NEFT INWARD CHARGES: FREE — no charge to the beneficiary (receiver)

RBI Waiver: In January 2020, RBI waived processing charges for member banks.
Banks were directed to pass these savings to customers.
Most major banks (SBI, HDFC, ICICI, Axis) now offer FREE online NEFT.
Branch NEFT may still carry the above charges.

NEFT TRANSACTION LIMITS:
  Minimum: ₹1 (no minimum limit)
  Maximum: No upper limit set by RBI
  Note: Individual banks may impose their own limits.
  Walk-in customers (non-account holders): ₹50,000 per transaction

NEFT INFORMATION REQUIRED:
  - Beneficiary name
  - Beneficiary account number
  - IFSC code of beneficiary bank
  - Bank name and branch

========================================
RTGS — REAL TIME GROSS SETTLEMENT
========================================
Full form: Real Time Gross Settlement
Operated by: Reserve Bank of India (RBI)
Settlement: Real-time (transaction by transaction, immediately)
Purpose: High-value transactions only
Availability: 24x7x365 (since December 14, 2020)

RTGS CHARGES — OUTWARD TRANSACTIONS:
  Transactions from ₹2,00,000 to ₹5,00,000: ₹24.50 + GST
  Transactions above ₹5,00,000             : ₹49.50 + GST

RTGS INWARD CHARGES: FREE — no charge to the beneficiary

RTGS TRANSACTION LIMITS:
  Minimum: ₹2,00,000 (₹2 lakh) — RTGS is only for high-value payments
  Maximum: No upper limit (as per RBI)
  Individual bank limits may apply.

KEY DIFFERENCE FROM NEFT:
  NEFT = batch processing every 30 minutes (good for regular payments)
  RTGS = instant settlement (good for large, urgent payments)
  Use RTGS when you need money to arrive immediately.
  Use NEFT when timing is not critical and amounts are smaller.

========================================
IMPS — IMMEDIATE PAYMENT SERVICE
========================================
Full form: Immediate Payment Service
Operated by: National Payments Corporation of India (NPCI)
Settlement: Real-time, 24x7x365
Purpose: Instant fund transfer at any amount

IMPS CHARGES (set by individual banks, not mandated by RBI):
  Up to ₹10,000       : ₹2.50 to ₹5.00 + GST (varies by bank)
  ₹10,001 to ₹1 lakh  : ₹5.00 to ₹15.00 + GST
  ₹1 lakh to ₹2 lakh  : ₹15.00 + GST (typically)
  Above ₹2 lakh        : ₹25.00 + GST (typically)
  Via mobile banking   : Often FREE (bank-specific)

NOTE: IMPS charges are bank-determined. Many banks offer free IMPS via mobile apps.
IMPS via USSD (*99#): ₹0.50 per transaction

IMPS TRANSACTION LIMITS:
  Minimum: ₹1
  Maximum: ₹5,00,000 (₹5 lakh) per transaction as per NPCI
  Some banks allow up to ₹2 lakh, others ₹5 lakh — check with your bank.

IMPS IDENTIFIERS:
  - MMID + Mobile number (mobile-to-mobile)
  - Account number + IFSC (account-to-account)
  - Aadhaar number (Aadhaar-linked)

IMPS vs NEFT vs RTGS COMPARISON:
  Feature         IMPS          NEFT          RTGS
  Settlement      Instant       30-min batch  Instant
  Min amount      ₹1            ₹1            ₹2,00,000
  Max amount      ₹5 lakh       No limit      No limit
  Availability    24x7          24x7          24x7
  Typical fee     ₹5-₹15+GST   ₹2.50-₹25+GST ₹24.50-₹49.50+GST
  Best for        Small-medium  Regular       High-value urgent

========================================
UPI — UNIFIED PAYMENTS INTERFACE
========================================
Full form: Unified Payments Interface
Operated by: NPCI
Settlement: Real-time

UPI CHARGES:
  Person-to-Person (P2P): FREE
  Person-to-Merchant (P2M): FREE (as per RBI and NPCI directives)
  Wallets to bank via UPI: FREE up to ₹2,000; ₹1 above ₹2,000 for PPIs

MDR (Merchant Discount Rate) on UPI:
  As of January 2020, RBI mandated ZERO MDR on UPI and RuPay transactions.
  Merchants cannot be charged for accepting UPI payments.

UPI TRANSACTION LIMITS:
  Standard: ₹1,00,000 per transaction
  UPI for capital markets: ₹2,00,000
  IPO applications: ₹5,00,000
  Medical/educational: ₹5,00,000

========================================
GST ON PAYMENT SERVICES
========================================
GST Rate on payment processing services: 18%
Applies to: NEFT fees, RTGS fees, IMPS fees, payment gateway fees
Does NOT apply to: The transaction amount itself (only on the service fee)

Example:
  NEFT transfer of ₹50,000 → Fee = ₹5 + 18% GST = ₹5.90 total charge

========================================
CROSS-BORDER / FOREX PAYMENTS
========================================
SWIFT charges (international wire transfers):
  Sending bank fee: ₹500 to ₹1,500 typically
  Correspondent bank charges: $15-$30 (deducted from amount)
  Currency conversion markup: 1% to 3.5% over mid-market rate

RBI guidelines on forex:
  Liberalized Remittance Scheme (LRS): Up to USD 2,50,000 per year per individual
  TCS (Tax Collected at Source): 20% on remittances above ₹7 lakh per year
  Form 15CA/15CB required for certain payments

How are payment fees calculated?
Stripe charges a per-transaction fee depending on the payment method used by the end customer.
More details on fees by payment method
.
Will I be charged tax?

More details on fees by payment method
.
Will I be charged tax?
Stripe is required to charge tax on certain products and services. Tax is charged based on your location and/ or your business/tax status, which determines whether a product or service is subject to taxes, and the applicable tax rates.
Read more
Are there additional fees for refunds?
For businesses on standard pricing: There may be
fees for refunds when bank transfers are used

For businesses on standard pricing: There may be
fees for refunds when bank transfers are used
.For all other payment methods, there are no fees for issuing refunds. The payment processing, connect and currency conversion fees from the original transaction are not returned.
For businesses on custom pricing: There may be fees for issuing refunds depending on your fee schedule with Stripe.
Do you have setup fees or monthly fees?
Stripe does not charge setup fees, monthly fees, or any other hidden fees like closure fees. All fees for businesses on standard pricing are listed on our website.

Do you offer any discounts?
Stripe offers custom pricing for businesses with large processing volumes. Eligibility varies by market.
